The Fed and BOJ are in between a rock and hard place going into next week's meetings.

For the Fed, oil +35% off its lows mean the bulk of the disinflation has likely past in these most recent prints' downside surprises. Warsh is a markets guy unlike his predecessor and is well aware of the message the bond market is sending right now - it ain't a good one. Its unruliness is a result of the reflationary vibe flowing back through markets and the only cure is combatting that via a more restrictive policy stance. My guess though is that he still tries to work this through balance sheet tightening rather than rate transmission, but we know the committee does not understand it the way he does so getting them on board will be his task.

In Japan, the BOJ faces the same problems listed above in terms of commodity inflation and bond market issues, but worse. The Yen is breaking down to new lows versus the dollar which exacerbates bond market unruliness and the structural inflation problem.

Both would benefit tremendously from a resolution to Middle East commodity supply chains so I wouldn't be surprise if Trump tries to TACO his way out before then. That said, the structural inflation pressures may be too strong and imbedded that it's not enough of a fix. Also sorta wild tariff noise is back in the mix again too. The cocktail of risks brewing is not healthy.

tweets.text:
"Less than 1% of global assets are onchain."

The other 99% is still sitting in traditional finance, waiting for infrastructure that can actually handle it.

That's what @alignedlayer is building for.

1️⃣ What is Aligned?

Aligned is a one-click stack that helps fintechs, institutions, and enterprises build financial products on Ethereum.

It brings the infrastructure businesses need to build on Ethereum together through one integration.

Basically, teams can use it to launch products like neobanks, stablecoin payment rails, and digital identity systems.

2️⃣ The problem it solves

Companies usually cannot build an Ethereum-based product using just one tool.

A fintech might need several different providers just to handle the infrastructure behind one product.

That means more integrations, higher costs, and more time spent managing different providers.

So instead of stitching all of that infrastructure together themselves, a team can use Aligned as the backend and focus more on building the actual product.

To me, it sounds like a good quality-of-life solution, in a space where things can get hectic fast.

3️⃣ How the stack works

The cool part is that Aligned handles most of the boring backend stuff while making the app feel like something you’re already used to using, like PayPal or Revolut.

You log in using Google or Face ID, and an Ethereum wallet is created for you in the background.

That is what Wallet-as-a-Service is designed for. Users get a real Ethereum wallet without needing seed phrases or browser extensions. They don't even need to hold ETH in their wallet because it supports gas sponsorship.

Aligned is also building Rollup-as-a-Service, which will let businesses run their product on a dedicated rollup with its own rules and infrastructure.

I love how they’re making this work like a normal fintech app without reminding you that you’re using crypto in the first place.

4️⃣ What is already live?

Aligned’s Proof Aggregation Service has been live in mainnet alpha since January 2026.

Instead of verifying every ZK proof separately on Ethereum, it combines multiple proofs into batches, which helps keep verification costs lower as activity grows.

So while some of Aligned’s other products are still being built, this part of the stack is already live and working.

I’ll def send another update when the rest goes live.

5️⃣ TGE

$ALIGN is the native token of the Aligned ecosystem.

It is an ERC-20 token on Ethereum with a fixed supply of 10B tokens and an initial circulating supply of approximately 16%.

You can check your airdrop eligibility here: https://t.co/Y1boxnAaWS

Also, make sure to follow @alignedlayer for official updates on the TGE and to see what other products they have in the pipeline.

NFA. DYOR.

Virtuals has already generated nearly $1.8M in fees this month.

That’s roughly 4x what it pulled in June, while VIRTUAL is only up around 8% over the past 30 days.

What's causing it? 

Robinhood Chain launched on July 1 with Virtuals’ AI-agent infrastructure integrated from day one. 

Since then, activity has accelerated quickly: the first $100M in agent volume took two weeks, while the next $50M took only three days. 

More than 4,500 agents have now launched with $2.3M raised for builders.

According to analytics, @Virtuals_io has become the second-largest fee generator on Robinhood Chain behind Uniswap, while generating 6.6x more fees there than on Base.

Those fees create real revenue for the Virtuals treasury, although the value accrual to VIRTUAL holders is still indirect.

Sooo why isn't the price going up?

The Iran situation and broader risk-off environment probably haven’t helped.

Anyways, when I see fees drastically increase but token price hasn't, it piques my curiousity.

That’s roughly 4x what it pulled in June, while VIRTUAL is only up around 8% over the past 30 days.

What's causing it? 

Robinhood Chain launched on July 1 with Virtuals’ AI-agent infrastructure integrated from day one. 

Since then, activity has accelerated quickly: the first $100M in agent volume took two weeks, while the next $50M took only three days. 

More than 4,500 agents have now launched with $2.3M raised for builders.

According to analytics, @Virtuals_io has become the second-largest fee generator on Robinhood Chain behind Uniswap, while generating 6.6x more fees there than on Base.

Those fees create real revenue for the Virtuals treasury, although the value accrual to VIRTUAL holders is still indirect.

Sooo why isn't the price going up?

The Iran situation and broader risk-off environment probably haven’t helped.

Anyways, when I see fees drastically increase but token price hasn't, it piques my curiousity.

tweets.text:
$TSLA earnings tonight. Here's the cheat sheet:

1. Auto Margins ex Credits: I expect auto gross margin ex-credits to be slightly above the Street's 18.5% because volumes were so strong. It was 19.2% in March. In theory, it should be flat quarter over quarter.

2. Deliveries: Key question is what drove the surge in June deliveries, up 25% y/y compared to up 6% in March? I believe high gas prices is a sustainable tailwind.

3. Capex. Here’s the negative wildcard. Last quarter, when the company guided capex to increase this year from $20B to $25B, it triggered a sell-off in after-hours trading, underscoring that the topic matters. I expect the company to guide to a number above $25B this year and give hints that 2027 should be above the Street’s $21B level.

4. Robotaxi. In July they added 3 cities and now operate small fleets in 7 cities. They’ve said two more cities coming, Las Vegas and Phoenix. I expect 3 more by year end, getting us to 10 in total. Street is expecting 2-3 more this year.

5. FSD. In March active paid subs grew 51% YY to 1.28m. In June I expect growth of 40%. The strong June delivery number should result in an acceleration in active sub growth in September because sales in the US come with one month free that acts as a top of funnel. FSD is approved in the Netherlands (April) and waiting for approval in the EU and China.

6. Cybercab: I expect Elon to move the target for production ramp from late 2026 to first half of 2027. My take: Investors are already expecting the slip.

7. Optimus. I’m not expecting much substance. Long term, 2040, this should be more than half of sales.

Toy department: SpaceX acquiring Tesla: We won’t get anything tonight on the topic. I put the odds at 80% that Tesla will be acquired by SpaceX within the next five years.

tweets.text:
𝗬𝗢𝗨𝗥 𝗦𝗧𝗢𝗣 𝗟𝗢𝗦𝗦 𝗜𝗦 𝗕𝗘𝗜𝗡𝗚 𝗛𝗨𝗡𝗧𝗘𝗗 𝗕𝗬 𝗔𝗜

I recently spoke with a friend who works at a quantitative fund.

I asked him about all the technical and Fibonacci levels that traders post every day on X.

He told me something very simple:

“Do you really think the algorithms are not aware of these levels?”

When thousands of traders watch the same support level, many of them place their stop losses in almost the same place, usually slightly below it.

The algorithms do not need to believe that the Fibonacci level has any magical meaning. They only need to understand where the orders are likely sitting.

As the price approaches the level, algorithms can anticipate the selling. Once the level breaks, stop losses are triggered, traders are forced out, and even more selling enters the market.

After that selling is exhausted, the same algorithms can buy into the liquidity and the price may quickly bounce back.

This is why the price so often falls slightly below the “perfect” technical level, stops everyone out, and then reverses.

It does not necessarily mean that one fund is controlling the market or illegally manipulating the price. It simply means that obvious levels create predictable human behavior, and quantitative funds are very good at taking advantage of predictable behavior.

David Sacks: Anthropic Wants to Ban Open Source AI in America

Listen to All-In to stay a few weeks (or months) ahead of everyone else

@DavidSacks back in May:

“I think where it's all leading to is an effort to ban open source models.

There's a lot of breadcrumbs leading here.

You look at a lot of the rhetoric around how models need to have guardrails, and that with open source models, the guardrails can be removed and therefore they're dangerous.

You see this rhetoric already in Anthropic's blog posts.

Any threat that they describe, they kind of go out of their way to take that shot at open source models.

They're trying to create ideas or put predicate facts in the public record to justify an action later on.

I think it's just a matter of time before they feel like they're at a position where maybe they can push for that type of ban directly.”

People are still debating whether AI takes your job. That's not the question. You can't compete with this intelligence at all. Next to it the human is negative value, the slowest one in the room.

And this is the slowest it will ever move again. It's about to run on its own, faster than we can conceive of, and nobody's prepared for that.

tweets.text:
Former Intel CEO explains what broke the company: We let the bean counters push out the technologists

@Jason:

“You spent a long time at Intel, 34 years. Probably one of the greatest American companies ever, and then absolutely went off the rails. What were the mistakes?”

Pat Gelsinger:

“I remember when I joined the executive staff for the first time, probably 15 of the 20 people that were in the room were PhDs. It was just that technical.

One of the things that went off the rails was when it started to be run by business people, as opposed to technical people.”

Jason:

“The bean counters, the finance people.”

@PGelsinger:

“Yeah. In the 5-6 years before I came back, Intel gave $100 billion to shareholders (in dividends and buybacks).

What I wouldn't have done for another $100 billion.

(Intel) hadn't built a new factory in a decade when I got there.

It's like, how can you not be building? How could you not buy EUV machines?

There's just all of these things that you would only do as a technologist because the economics behind them by themselves were not good.

If you look at the great technology companies today, they're deeply technical… And when you're making these hardcore technical decisions that affect billions of dollars, you don't do that through a spreadsheet.”

tweets.text:
Congrats to my friend @eisokant for a superb model! 2 things imo are really exciting about this @poolsideai release.

1/ the model is extremely cheap and efficient. Laguna S 2.1 has 118B total params but only 8B active per token. It already costs basically nothing to run in the cloud, and more importantly, this is the kind of model that can increasingly run locally on a laptop or device.

That means private, low-latency agents running all day without sending everything to a giant data center. Coding, research, personal assistants, robots, etc. Intelligence too cheap to meter will probably look less like one massive model in the cloud and more like millions of smaller models running everywhere (remember the next Surface Pro laptop will have a Nvidia GPU)

2/ Poolside seems to have built a real model factory.

They trained this on 4,096 H200s and went from the start of pretraining to launch in under 9 weeks. Obviously 4,096 GPUs is not small lol, but compared with frontier-scale training runs, it is incredibly lean.

And they are shipping fast. Three models in three months. Same pretraining data, fast recipe iteration, better post-training, long RL rollouts, multiple harnesses, FP8 RL, and hundreds of thousands of software and terminal environments.

The loop is impressive. It’s like Train a model, evaluate it, inspect the trajectories, improve the data and harness, launch again a few weeks later. That kind of iteration speed compounds like crazy and will accelerate the price drop of smaller models while quality will increase. 

The frontier labs will keep spending billions to push the ceiling. But there may also be enormous value in factories that can repeatedly produce smaller, cheaper, highly specialized models that run locally and improve every few weeks.
tweets.quoted_tweet_text:
Today we are releasing Laguna S 2.1.

At 118B total parameters, with 8B active per token, it does the work of models several times its size on agentic coding. It is remarkably persistent across long-horizon tasks. And it is small enough to run on a single NVIDIA DGX Spark.
